content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Mr. Bruce Buel 
General Manager 
Administration 
322-5660 
Monterey Peninsula Water 
Management District 
P.O. Box 85 
Monterey, CA 
93940 
Re: 
No. A-82-066 
Dear Mr. Buel: 
Executive/Legal 
•• 
Enforcement 
322-5901 
322-64.41 
May 18, 1982 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your letter requesting advice from this 
office. 
My understanding is that you asked the questions 
contained in your letter on behalf of the Board of the 
Monterey Peninsula Water Management District. 
The first question you asked is whether state law 
permits the District Chairman or the Board as a whole to 
require a Board member to disqualify him or herself on a 
decision if the Chairman or Board feels that the director 
has a conflict of interest. 
My answer is limited to the 
situation where the Chairman or Board feels there is a 
conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, 
Government Code Sections 81000, et seq.!/ 
What constitutes a conflict of interest under the 
Political Reform Act is often a difficult determination. 
Normally an official determines for him or herself, often 
with the help of counsel, whether he or she has a conflict 
and acts accordingly. 
The Act provides for immunity from 
civil or criminal penalties for an official who acts 
pursuant to an opinion from the Commission. 
Section 
83114(a). 
There is more limited immunity for an official 
who acts pursuant to written advice from Commission staff. 
Section 83114(b). 
!I All statutory references made are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise noted. 
There are other 
laws which deal with conflicts of interest; this office is 
authorized to interpret and advise as to the requirements 
of the Political Reform Act only. 


Mr. Bruce B 
May 18, 198 
Page 2 
The Act provides civil, criminal and administrative 
sanctions for violations of the disqualification 
provisions. 
Sections 91000, et seq. 
It does not authorize 
the agency involved to restrain or prohibit the 
participation of art official in a decision on the ground 
that the agency believes the official has a conflict of 
interest. 
However, an agency may invoke its own 
disciplinary procedures if an official violates the 
agency's conflict of interest code. 
Section 91003.5. 
The 
Act also provides for judicial action in the form of 
injunctive relief to prevent an official from participating 
in a decision in which he or she has a conflict of 
interest. 
Section 91003(a). 
Injunctive relief is the only 
means by which an official's participation in a decision 
when he or she has a conflict of interest may be 
prohibited. 
The second question you raised is whether the Board 
incurs any liability if a Board member votes and is then 
later found to have had a conflict of interest in the 
matter. 
My answer is again limited to the situation where 
the member has a conflict of interest under the Political 
Reform Act. 
The Act does not place liability for 
violations of the disqualification provisions on the agency 
involved. 
The liability for any violations of the Act is 
personal to the official involved. 
The Act does allow a 
court to enjoin an official action upon a showing that a 
violation of the disqualification provision has occurred. 
A court may also set aside a decision if it determines that 
a violation did occur, that the official action might not 
otherwise have been taken or approved, and that innocent 
persons who relied on the official action will not suffer. 
Section 91003(b). 
I trust that this discussion has been helpful. 
If I 
can be of further assistance, please feel free to contact 
me at 916/322-5901. 
DMF: km 
cc: 
David Laredo 
MPWMD Board Members 
McClintock, Chair 
Lee 
Woodworth 
Henson 
Fry 
Gawthrop 
Peters 
Very truly yours, 
.~ 
~. 
/kti~;aVv'L~~~ 
Diane MaurVFishburn 
~ 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


~ * MONTEREY 
PENINSULA 
'b'WATER MANAGEMENT DISTRICT 
187 Eldorado. Suite E • P.O. Box 85 • Monterey, CA 93940 • (408) 649-4866 
April 13, 1982 
State of California Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Gentlemen: 
Several questions concerning the State's Conflict of Interest Laws have 
arisen before the District Board that I would appreciate your cooperation 
in answering. 
(1) 
Specifically, does the state law allow for anyone, aside from an in-
dividual who may have a conflict, to make a determination on the conflict? 
At issue here is whether or not the District Chairman or the Board as a 
whole can require a director to step down from a decision if the Chairman 
or the District Board feels that a conflict exists. 
(2) 
Does the District Board carry any liability in a circumstance where 
a District Board Member has participated or voted and is subsequently 
found to have a conflict of interest? 
The District does not require a formal oplnlon on either of these ques-
tions, but would appreciate your interpretation of the state law on these 
matters. 
I 
II Bruce Buel 
General Manager 
BB:g 
cc: 
David Laredo, District Counsel 
MPWMD Board Members

sections.question: 1) Does state law allow for anyone, aside from an individual who may have a conflict, to make a determination on the conflict? Specifically, whether the District Chairman or the Board as a whole can require a director to step down from a decision if the Chairman or the District Board feels that a conflict exists. 2) Does the District Board carry any liability in a circumstance where a District Board Member has participated or voted and is subsequently found to have a conflict of interest?
sections.conclusion: The Act does not authorize the agency involved to restrain or prohibit the participation of an official in a decision on the ground that the agency believes the official has a conflict of interest. However, an agency may invoke its own disciplinary procedures if an official violates the agency's conflict of interest code. Injunctive relief through judicial action is the only means by which an official's participation in a decision when he or she has a conflict of interest may be prohibited. The Act does not place liability for violations of the disqualification provisions on the agency involved; liability is personal to the official involved. A court may set aside a decision if it determines a violation occurred and other conditions are met.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asks whether the Board can compel a member to disqualify themselves due to a perceived conflict of interest, and whether the Board faces liability if a member votes despite having an undisclosed conflict of interest under California's Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under the Political Reform Act, a Board cannot unilaterally require a member to disqualify themselves; only judicial injunctive relief can prohibit participation based on a conflict. The Board itself bears no liability for a member's conflict of interest violation; liability is personal to the individual official, though a court may set aside the decision under specified circumstances.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a complete FPPC advice letter response with both incoming request and FPPC counsel response. Both question and conclusion sections are clearly identified in the FPPC response. Document ID is A-82-066 (or 82A066 as provided). Requestor is Bruce Buel, General Manager of Monterey Peninsula Water Management District.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) Does state law allow for anyone, aside from an individual who may have a conflict, to make a determination on the conflict? Specifically, whether the District Chairman or the Board as a whole can require a director to step down from a decision if the Chairman or the District Board feels that a conflict exists. 2) Does the District Board carry any liability in a circumstance where a District Board Member has participated or voted and is subsequently found to have a conflict of interest?

CONCLUSION: The Act does not authorize the agency involved to restrain or prohibit the participation of an official in a decision on the ground that the agency believes the official has a conflict of interest. However, an agency may invoke its own disciplinary procedures if an official violates the agency's conflict of interest code. Injunctive relief through judicial action is the only means by which an official's participation in a decision when he or she has a conflict of interest may be prohibited. The Act does not place liability for violations of the disqualification provisions on the agency involved; liability is personal to the official involved. A court may set aside a decision if it determines a violation occurred and other conditions are met.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel Diane Maur responds to the Monterey Peninsula Water Management District's questions about whether the Board can require members to disqualify due to conflicts of interest and whether the Board faces liability if a member votes despite having a conflict. The response explains that only judicial injunctive relief can prohibit participation, that liability is personal to the official (not the agency), and that courts may set aside decisions meeting certain conditions.
